coredata.dc:title: Effect of different carbon dots positions on the transfer of photo-induced charges in type I heterojunction for significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Hao, Chenchen
Shi, Yuxing
Guo, Feng
Tang, Yubin
coredata.dc:description: The design of highly efficient photocatalysts is a hot topic in the field of photocatalysis. Herein, two kinds of ternary heterojunctions with coating carbon dots (CDs) on the different positions of ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 heterojunctions (CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 and ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4-CDs) were developed for exploring the role of CDs on charge transfer of heterojunctions. Compared with ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4-CDs (CDs are fixed to the exterior of the core–shell structure), CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 (CDs are fixed to the interior of the core–shell structure) exhibits a more efficient acceleration of migration and separation of photo-induced charges. As expected, the optimal CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 sample exhibits the optimized tetracycline (TC, 30 mg/L) degradation rate of 87.3 % in 120 min under visible light irradiation. This work reveals that CDs existing at the contact interface of heterojunction establish smooth and fast channels for electron transport, accelerate electron transport and prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus significantly improving the photocatalytic performance of type-I heterojunction constructed by ZnFe2O4 and ZnIn2S4.

head.ce:title: Effect of different carbon dots positions on the transfer of photo-induced charges in type I heterojunction for significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of highly efficient photocatalysts is a hot topic in the field of photocatalysis. Herein, two kinds of ternary heterojunctions with coating carbon dots (CDs) on the different positions of ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 heterojunctions (CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 and ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4-CDs) were developed for exploring the role of CDs on charge transfer of heterojunctions. Compared with ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4-CDs (CDs are fixed to the exterior of the core–shell structure), CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 (CDs are fixed to the interior of the core–shell structure) exhibits a more efficient acceleration of migration and separation of photo-induced charges. As expected, the optimal CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 sample exhibits the optimized tetracycline (TC, 30 mg/L) degradation rate of 87.3 % in 120 min under visible light irradiation. This work reveals that CDs existing at the contact interface of heterojunction establish smooth and fast channels for electron transport, accelerate electron transport and prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus significantly improving the photocatalytic performance of type-I heterojunction constructed by ZnFe2O4 and ZnIn2S4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of antibiotics is a milestone in the history of human medicine, which brings infinite dawn to human medicine. Nevertheless, the abuse of antibiotics and the refractory nature of the antibiotics themselves lead to the emergence of environmental problems with antibiotics and their residues detected in different aqueous environments [1–3]. Photocatalytic technology has aroused worldwide research interests concerned by researchers because it can exploit sunlight to degrade refractory organic pollutants [4]. So far, various semiconductor photocatalysts (such as TiO2 [5,6], g-C3N4 [7,8], CdS [9,10], etc) have been developed for removal of antibiotics in water bodies. However, individual semiconductor photocatalysts have disadvantages such as low activity, weak visible light absorption capacity, and fast recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes [11–13].

To address this problem, some studies have proved that constructing heterojunctions is a commonly used and effective method [5,14]. Low et al [15]. reported that there are three types of conventional heterojunction: straddling gap (type I), staggered gap (type II) and broken gap (type III) (See Scheme S1). Compared with photocatalytic mechanisms of type II and type III heterojunctions, type I heterojunction photocatalyst exhibits obviously weaker reduced redox capacity. In the type-I heterojunction photocatalysis system, the photo-generated electrons generated by the semiconductor with more negative conduction band (CB) potential migrate to the more positive semiconductor. Synchronously, the photo-generated holes migrate from the semiconductor with more positive valence band (VB) potential to the more negative semiconductor. Transfer path of the electrons described above weakens its redox ability, and then leads to the general photocatalytic activity [16]. Another reason for the low activity of this type is that the recombination rate of electron and hole is too fast. This is because the excited electrons and holes on the semiconductor with broadband gap are gathered in the narrow band gap semiconductor, increasing the recombination probability of photo-generated electrons and holes on the narrow band gap semiconductor, leading to the general photocatalytic activity [17]. Although a number of studies with type I heterojunctions have been developed, such as CdS/ZnS [18], GaN/g-C3N4 [19], red phosphorus/g-C3N4 [20], it remains challenging to search a simple way to further suppress the photo-generated electron and hole recombination of type-I heterojunction, which provides more possibilities for reasonable design for improving the activity of photocatalyst.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a newly emerging kind of carbon-based material, possess many unique natures, such as up-converted photoluminescence behavior, brilliant visible responses, and electron transfer and reservoir properties [21–24], which make CDs-based composites a research hotspot in the field of photocatalysis. Recently, the combination of heterojunctions with solid-state electronic media can effectively reduce the resistance between contact interfaces and accelerate the migration and separation of photogenerated carriers, a modification strategy to effectively enhance the activity of heterojunctions [25,26]. Compared with metal-based solid-state electronic media, CDs has the advantages of low cost, non-toxic and easy synthesis [27–29], which becomes a shining nova in heterojunction modification. For instance, Zhang et al. prepared the trinary CDs/SrTiO3/NH4V4O10 photocatalyst by coating CDs on the surface of Z-Scheme heterojunction, where CDs act as the bridge of charge carriers, facilitating the transfer and separation of photoinduced charge carriers and significantly improving the photocatalytic activity of the composite [30]. Nevertheless, most reports have only discussed the constructs restricted to coupling CDs with Z-scheme or type II heterojunctions, and there are no relevant reports about the construction of ternary heterojunction of combing CDs with type I heterojunctions. Meanwhile, few studies have investigated the effects of CDs at different locations on the activity of the modified heterojunctions. As an example, we preliminary designed ternary composites with decorating CDs at different positions on the ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 type I heterojunction to study the above problems.

In this work, ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4, CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 and ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4-CDs with core–shell structures were synthesized (the synthesis process diagram is shown in Scheme 1 ) were synthesized, and the effects of CDs in the type I heterojunction on the activity were discussed. The experimental results demonstrated that compared with ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 and ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4-CDs photocatalyst, the photocatalytic degradation antibiotic activity of CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 photocatalyst is significantly improved. This revealed that CDs located at the contact interface of ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 type I heterojunction with core–shell structure (located at the interior of core–shell structure), as an efficient transfer medium for photo-generated electrons, which provides a high-speed and smooth channel for electron migration, effectively realizing the separation of photo-excited electrons and holes.

The carbon dots (CDs) solution was synthesized from fresh folium ginkgo via a simple hydrothermal method [31,32]. Primarily, the folium ginkgo (8 g) was putted into ultra-pure water (150 mL), and then transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave. The autoclave was placed in an oven and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. After cooling, the obtained product was filtered with a mixed cellulose ester membrane (0.22 μm), then the brownish-yellow CDs solution with the concentration of 11 mg/mL was obtained and stored at 4 ℃ until used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnFe2O4 and CDs/ZnFe2O4 microspheres (route Ⅱ and route III)
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnFe2O4 microspheres were prepared via a solvothermal method [33]. First, Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (1 mmol) and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (2 mmol) were dispersed in a mixed solvent of isopropanol (60 mL) and glycerol (16 mL). After ultrasonic and stirring for half an hour, respectively, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave. Then, the autoclave was placed in an oven and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. Next, the emerald precipitate was washed alternately with water and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the dried product was calcined in air at 400 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C/min, and the brick-red ZnFe2O4 microspheres were successfully prepared. In addition, in the precursor solution of preparation of ZnFe2O4, different amounts of CDs solution (350, 700 and 1050 μL) were added, and stirring for another 5 min, the CDs/ZnFe2O4 composites was prepared by the same experimental steps for synthesizing ZnFe2O4. For convenience, the ZnFe2O4 and CDs/ZnFe2O4 composites were denoted as ZFO and xC-ZFO, where × was the dosages (x = 1, 2 and 3 correspond to 350, 700 and 1050 μL, respectively) of CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnIn2S4 wrapped CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite (route Ⅳ)
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnIn2S4 wrapped CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite was synthesized by a low temperature hydrothermal method. In detail, different amount of CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite was added to a mixed solution of glycerol (8 mL) and ultra-pure water (32 mL, pH 2.5 (adjust by HCl 1 mol/L)). After stirring for 30 min at room temperature, 0.272 mg ZnCl2, 0.586 mg of InCl3·4H2O, and 0.3 mg of thioacetamide (TAA) was added to the above solution, and stirred continuously for 30 min. Then, the mixture was put into a water bath at 80 °C under stirring for 2 h. Finally, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, and washed with ethanol for several times and dried at 60 °C, resulting in ZnIn2S4 wrapped CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite with different CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite additions (the weight ratios of CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite to ZnIn2S4 were 20, 30, and 40 wt%). For facilitate reading, the products were abbreviated as y-xC-ZFO@ZIS, where y represent the quality ratio of CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite to ZnIn2S4. In contrast, pure ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) was obtained by the same processes without the adding of CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 and ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4@CDs (route Ⅴ and route Ⅵ)
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of comparison, the ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 and ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4@CDs composite was synthesized by a low temperature hydrothermal method. The ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 (abbreviate ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 to ZFO@ZIS) was obtained by the same processes as y-xC-ZFO@ZIS, with the only difference is that CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite was replaced by ZnFe2O4. The ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4@CDs composite was synthesized by the same processes as ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4, with the only difference is that CDs solution was added when heated in 80 ℃ water bath for 30 min. For facilitate reading, the products were abbreviated as ZFO@ZIS@CDs.
Detailed characterizations, photodegradation processes, active species capture experiments, and degradation product analysis were given in the Supporting Information .

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out to analysis the phase compositions of as-prepared samples. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the peaks at 2θ values of 21.1°, 27.7°, 47.5°, 52.4° and 56.4° are corresponded to (006), (102), (110), (116), (022) crystallographic planes of the hexagonal phase ZIS (JCPDS No.65–2023) [34]. The diffraction peaks at 30.1°, 35.3°, 43.0°, 53.3°, 56.8° and 62.3° can be indexed to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of cubic spinel ZFO (JCPDS:01–077-0011), respectively [35–37]. With respect to the 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS sample, the predominant characteristic signals of ZFO and ZIS can be found, which indicated that the composite photocatalyst has been successfully synthesized. Moreover, the similar results of XRD pattern over 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C can also be obtained. Nevertheless, no obvious characteristic peaks of CDs were observed, probably due to the relatively limited amount [38]. Furthermore, in order to convenience of comparison, the XRD patterns of ZFO, C-ZFO composites and 30-ZFO@ZIS were shown in Fig.S1, which also shows the composition of each composite material. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were examined to further confirmed the functional groups on resultant material surfaces. In Fig.S2, the peaks at 550 and 3446 cm−1 of ZFO and all C-ZFO composites are assigned to the stretching vibrations of Zn-O modes and OH [34]. For C-ZFO composites, the peak at 1401 and 1639 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC and CO, demonstrating the presence of carbon species (such as CDs) in the composite [31,35]. As exhibited in Fig. 1b, the characteristic peaks of Zn-O, CC and CO can be observed in 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS and 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C composites, which can also further prove the successful synthesis of the composite material.

The morphology of the synthesized samples was observed though the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). In Fig.S3, pure ZIS shows the flower-like microsphere structure with agglomerated ultrathin nanosheets, which is consistent with previous report [38]. As for pure ZFO (Fig.S4), the ZFO clearly shows microspheres hollow structure with a diameter about 1 μm, while the 2C-ZFO sample still maintains a spherical hollow structure (Fig. S5a-c), but its diameter is slightly reduced compared with ZFO, which can be attributed to the slight change of solvent affecting the dimension of ZFO [39]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of 2C-ZFO in Fig.S5d exhibits the CDs with the size of around 2–5 nm were uniformly deposited on the surface of ZFO without particulate aggregation. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is close to the (100) planar spacing of graphitic carbon, which agrees with previous study[40]. In addition, lattice stripes with relatively high contrast and lattice spacing of 0.248 nm are assigned to the (311) plane of ZFO [41]. The above results revealed that the CDs are evenly dispersed on ZFO rather than congregated on the surface. After the in-situ low temperature hydrothermal reaction, ZIS nanosheets are evenly distributed on the surface of the 2C-ZFO microspheres (Fig.S6). Meanwhile, it can be seen from the Fig. 2 a of individual broken 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS that internal hollow structure of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS can be clearly observed. The hollow microstructure (Fig. 2b) of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS was further examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Obviously, in Fig. 2c, it is not hard to find that the hollow structure with wrapping of dense ZIS nanosheets remains maintained. Moreover, the HRTEM image of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS composite was given in Fig. 2d, the lattice spacing of 0.21, 0.248 and 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, (311) plane of ZFO and the (102) plane of ZIS, respectively [41,42]. Fig. 2e displays the corresponding elemental mapping images of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS, further revealing the homogeneous distribution of C, Fe, O, Zn, In and S in 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS composite. Furthermore, the SEM images of 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C and 30-ZFO@ZIS were taken and provided in Fig.S7a and Fig.S8, both basically maintain the microspheres’ structure, which shows that the spherical shape of ZFO cannot be destroyed after the growth of ZIS nanosheets via the low temperature hydrothermal reaction. Fig.S7b and c presented the TEM image of 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C, and it can be seen clearly that CDs distributed evenly on the surface of ZIS nanosheets, which wraps on the ZFO microspheres. The above results proved the different positions of CDs in 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS and 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C composites, in which 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS composite, the CDs were located on the surface of ZFO as the core for 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS composite, while the CDs were fixed on the surface of the outer ZIS nanosheets of 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C composite.

The chemical compositions, chemical states and constituent elements (i.e., Fe, Zn, In, S, C and O) of ZFO, ZIS and 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS). First, Fig.S9 shows the survey spectra of as-prepared samples, demonstrating the coexistent of Fe, C, Zn, In, S, and O elements in 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS composite. In Fig. 3 a, the two main peaks of ZFO at 710.3 and 723.9 eV can be attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, which explained that the oxidation state of Fe ions is + 3 in ZFO [43]. Furthermore, two satellite peaks are observed at about 717.5 and 732.0 eV, which are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Fe3+ [44]. Moreover, the binding energy of Fe elements in the 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS composite decreases compared to ZFO, which reveal a strong interface interaction between 2C-ZFO and ZIS [44]. The characteristic peaks centered at 529.7 and 531.1 eV in the O 1 s spectra of Fig. 3b are ascribed to Fe-O and Zn-O bonds [43], and after the combination of 2C-ZFO and ZIS, Zn-O bonds occupies the main position. Fig. 3c shows the C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra, and the peaks at 284.6, 285.2, and 289.0 eV are ascribed to CC, CO and C-CO groups of CDs in 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS [31]. In Fig. 3d, the XPS spectra of Zn elements in ZFO can be split into two peaks with banding energies of 1021.3 and 1044.3 eV, corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, which indicated the Zn(II) oxidation state of ZFO [45]. Similarly, it can be seen that the chemical state of Zn is + 2 in ZIS and 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS. As displayed in Fig. 3e, two peaks of In 3d centered at 445.2 (In 3d5/2) and 452.7 eV (In 3d3/2) are corresponded to the characteristic peak of In3+ [11,46–48]. The S 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum for30-2C-ZFO@ZIS in Fig. 3f, two peaks at 161.8 and 163.2 eV can be assigned to the S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 orbitals of S2− ions [49,50]. Compared with pure ZIS, the binding energy of Zn, In and S elements in 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS shifts toward left or right, which is attributed to the electron transfer caused by the formation of heterojunction interface [51].

The photo-absorption performance of different samples was investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the pure ZFO possessed relatively wide absorption band in the visible-light region. Meanwhile, the pure ZIS presented an absorption edge at about 500 nm, whereas a redshift at the absorption edge in the visible-light region is observed after combining ZIS and 2C-ZFO to form a composite photocatalyst. In addition, the light absorption range of 2C-ZFO@ZIS composites increases with the increasing of 2C-ZFO content owing to the formation of the 2C-ZFO@ZIS heterostructure [52]. Next, tetracycline (TC) is selected as a degradation contaminant to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig.S10a, after introducing CDs, both the adsorption activity and the photocatalytic degradation activity of ZFO on TC were improved. This may be due to CDs enriched in oxygen-containing functional groups that favor the adsorption of TC. Meanwhile, CDs have excellent electron transfer properties, which facilitates the transfer of photogenerated electrons [53,54]. Among them, when the dosage of CDs solution is 700 μL (named as 2C-ZFO), the photocatalytic degradation effect for TC is optimal and the degradation processes of all C-ZFO samples are compounded with the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics (Fig.S10b). Similarly, by adjusting the dosage of 2C-ZFO, the optimum material ratio of 2C-ZFO to ZIS in the composite catalyst can be determined. As displayed in Fig.S10c, compared with pure ZIS (degradation rate of TC 49.9 %) and ZFO (degradation rate of TC 54.3 %), the 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS (the mass ratio of 2C-ZFO to ZIS is 30 %,) possesses best photocatalytic degradation activity, and the photocatalytic degradation of TC for all samples follows the pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig.S10d). Interestingly, it found that the position of CDs in the ZFO@ZIS composites has a certain influence on the photocatalytic activity of the composite photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 4b, compared with 30-ZFO@ZIS, 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C showed slightly improved photocatalytic activity, which was attributed to the contribution of CDs. However, 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS displayed the most optimal photocatalytic performance, and the final TC removal rate reached 87.3 %, which may be due to the different positions of CDs changed the photo-induced electron transfer path, and then affected the photocatalytic activity of composite materials. The result reflects the photocatalytic activity of the compound of ZFO@ZIS and CDs is better when the CDs are in the surface of ZFO than when the CDs are located on the surface of ZIS nanosheets. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation of TC in all samples follows pseudo-first order reaction kinetics (Fig. 4c), and the kinetic constants are provided in Fig. 4d. Noted that the TC degradation performance of as-prepared CDs-ZnFe2O4@ZnIn2S4 is higher than that of most research works about representative photocatalysts in recent reports (Table.S1). The stability of photocatalyst is a crucial factor to determine whether it can be put into practical application. Thus, the stability of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS photocatalysts in degradation of TC was studied through four repeated experiments, and the results are shown in Fig. 4e. After four repeated experiments, the degradation efficiency of TC with 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS under visible light decreased from 83 % to 74 %. In addition, the XRD pattern of used 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS was performed and given in Fig. 4f, implying there is no distinct change in the crystal structure of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS after photocatalysis. The above results verified that the outstanding stability of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS for the degradation of TC. Meanwhile, the SEM image of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS after reaction was exhibited in Fig.S11. Obviously, the morphology and structure of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS were also not destroyed after photocatalytic reaction.

In addition, the mineralization degree of TC solution degraded by different catalysts for 120 min was detected and shown in Fig. 5 a. Under the presence of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS, the removal rate of total organic carbon (TOC) after photocatalytic degradation of TC is as high as 69.8 %. Obviously, 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS photocatalyst has an excellent mineralization effect on the photocatalytic degradation of TC [55]. Fig. 5b reveals the influence of different pH environments on TC degradation. Typically, 1 M HCl or 1 M NaOH was used to adjust the pH of the solution, the initial pH value of solution without adjustment was 7.0. It can be seen from Fig. 5b that both acidic and alkaline environments have adverse effects on TC degradation, and alkaline environment has a more obvious inhibitory effect on TC degradation. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that acidic environment is more conducive to the production of •OH radicals (see Eqs. (1–4)) [25,56]. In addition, while adjusting the pH, the introduced Cl- ions can consume •OH radicals (see Eq. (5)), so the acidic environment slightly inhibits the degradation process of TC [57]. Overall, the 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS shows outstanding TC degradation performance in a wide pH range, indicating that the system is beneficial to treat wastewater with a wide pH range. Fig. 5e indicates the influence of different water bodies on TC degradation, and the sampling places of river water and tap water were shown in Fig. 5c and d. Evidently, river water has the most significant inhibition effect on TC degradation, followed by tap water. Moreover, the adsorption effect of TC in this water body becomes worse, which may be the main reason for the reduction of TC degradation rate. Nonetheless, this experimental result also proves that the photocatalytic degradation of TC in different water bodies has not been seriously disturbed. The influence of common anions (Cl-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-) in water on photocatalytic degradation of TC is illustrated in Fig. 5f. Notably, all anions of Cl-, NO3 –, CO3 2– and SO4 2- have slight adverse effects on the catalytic reaction at a concentration of 5 mM. Among them, the effect of NO3 – on TC degradation was the least significant. And the Cl- ions can consume •OH radicals (see Eq. (5)). Moreover, the SO4 2- ions can react with •OH radicals (see Eqs. (6–7)) [58]. The CO3 2– can inhibit the degradation of TC because of the following reasons: on the one hand, CO3 2– can change the pH of the solution and make it alkaline; on the other hand, CO3 2– can be used as a scavenger of •OH radicals (see Eq. (8)) [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced analysis of the photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of ZIS, ZFO and 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS are given in Fig. 6 a. Significantly, the specific surface areas of ZIS, ZFO, 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS are 43, 84 and 178 m2 g−1, respectively, which is consistent with the adsorption results measured in the degradation process. In order to explore the recombination of photo-generated carriers, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the prepared catalysts were performed under excitation wavelength of 345 nm. As can be clearly observed from Fig.S12a, 2C-ZFO composite exhibited a lower PL intensity than pure ZFO, indicated that the recombination of photoexcited electron and hole was effectively suppressed due to the electron transfer property of CDs. Meanwhile, the PL spectra (Fig. 6b) displayed that 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS has the weakest fluorescence signal intensity, which indicates that the position of CDs that encapsulated between ZFO and ZIS plays a more effective role in preventing the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs compared with the CDs existing on the surface of the outer ZIS. Fig.S12b and Fig. 6c displays the transient photocurrent response of all catalysts. Due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, the pure ZFO and ZIS showed the weakest light response signal [60]. The photo-response signal of C-ZFO composites increases after the introduction of CDs, suggesting that the CDs is beneficial to the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers [61]. Contrasting with 30-ZFO@ZIS and 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C, 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS exhibited a more stronger photocurrent density owing to the fact that the CDs loaded in the cavity and surface of ZFO, which promote the separation and migration of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [62]. Fig.S12c and Fig. 6d discloses the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) changes of as-prepared catalysts, and the illustration in Fig. 6d shows the corresponding equivalent Randle circuit after fitting Nyquist plots. From Fig.S12c, it can be clearly observed that pure ZFO possesses a biggest semicircle diameter, and the diameter decreases after introducing CDs. The 2C-ZFO has the smallest semicircle diameter, suggesting the introduction of CDs plays a positive role in improving the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers [63]. Furthermore, compared with 30-ZFO@ZIS and 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C, 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS had the smallest semicircle diameter, revealing that the electron transfer resistance is the smallest when the CDs is between ZFO and ZIS heterojunction interface. For a more intuitive understanding of the migration of photo-generated carriers, the time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) of the 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS and 30-ZFO@ZIS@2C was detected (Fig. S13). The 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS (1.71 ns) exhibits a longer lifetime than 30-ZFO@ZIS@2C (1.15 ns), which indicated that 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS could availably increase the separation efficiency of photo-induced carriers.

By adding different kinds of sacrificial agents in the process of photocatalytic degradation of TC, the active species produced in the reaction process were revealed. The 1,4-Benzoquinone (1,4-BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and ammonium oxalate (AO) were used to capture superoxide radicals (•O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and holes (h+ ), respectively [64,65]. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the addition of IPA inhibits the degradation of TC, which indicated that •OH play a part in the photocatalytic degradation of TC with 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C. •O2 − and h+ play the leading role in the TC degradation process over 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C [66]. The results of capturing experiment in the presence of 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS are shown in Fig. 7b. Obviously, h+ and •O2 − still maintain major active roles in this complex system, which indicates that the main roles of active species were not changed in the system of anchoring CDs at different positions of the heterojunctions. In addition, the electronic spin resonance (ESR) technique was performed to further confirm the existence of active species. Fig. S14 displayed the ESR signals of TEMPO − h+ for 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS and 30-ZFO@ZIS@2C. Obviously, 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS exhibits the stronger ESR signal intensity of TEMPO − h+ . Moreover, from Fig. 7c, it is clearly obvious that the 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C exhibits strong ESR signal strength of DMPO−•O2 − and DMPO−•OH. The results of ESR indicated the TC degradation process with 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C produced h+ , •O2 − and •OH radicals [67], and h+ are the main active species in photocatalytic reaction. Compared with 30-ZFO@ZIS-2C, the 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS displayed stronger signal intensities of TEMPO − h+ , DMPO−•O2 − and DMPO−•OH (Fig. 7d), indicating the different positions of CDs in heterojunction can effectively increase the number of active free radicals based on the different transfer paths of photo-induced electrons, thus affecting the photocatalytic activity of composite materials. In other words, CDs located at the interface of the two-phase heterojunction favors the separation of electrons and holes.

Based on the Kubelka-Munk (K-M) equation, the band gap energies (Eg ) of ZFO and ZIS could be calculated. As displayed in Fig. 8 a, the Eg values of ZFO and ZIS were measured to be 1.93 eV and 2.62 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the Mott-Schottky plots of ZIS and ZFO were displayed in Fig. 8b. Obviously, ZIS and ZFO both are typical n-type semiconductors due to the positive slopes, and the flat-band potentials of ZIS and ZFO are −0.95 and −0.54 eV vs SCE [68]. Due to the conduction band (CB) edge potential of n-type semiconductor is approximately equivalent to the flat-band potential. Thus, the CB potentials of ZIS and ZFO are −0.95 and −0.54 eV [69,70]. Based on the empirical formulae of EVB = ECB + Eg
[71], the corresponding valence band (VB) values of ZFO and ZIS were calculated to be 1.39 and 1.67 eV, respectively. In order to the clarify the influence of the different positions of CDs more clearly, the migration and separation of photoexcited electrons (e-) and holes (h+ ) were systematically proposed and analyzed. As illustrated in Fig.S15, photo-excited e- and h+ can be generated in the CB and VB of ZFO and ZIS under visible light irradiation. According to the band gap structures of ZIS and ZFO, it is judged that the heterojunction type formed in ZFO@ZIS composite photocatalyst belongs to type I heterojunction, and the e- and h+ in the CB and VB of ZIS tend to transfer to the that of ZFO. For ZFO@ZIS, due to the e- and h+ both accumulate on the surface of ZFO, the separation effect of e- and h+ is not ideal, which leads to the limitation of the improvement of photocatalytic activity. For ZFO@ZIS-C composite photocatalyst, after decorating CDs on the surface of outer ZIS nanosheets, the heterojunction type between ZFO and ZIS still is the type I heterostructure (Fig. 8c). Compared with ZFO@ZIS, CDs on the surface of outer ZIS are helpful to promote a small part of e- transfer to the surface of CDs due to the excellent electron storage capacity of CDs, which is beneficial to improve photocatalytic activity. As for the catalyst of C-ZFO@ZIS (Fig. 8d), the CDs are located at the type I heterojunction interface between ZFO and ZIS. Electrons tend to move to positions with lower potential energy. Therefore, compared with the transfer from ZIS to CDs in ZFO@ZIS-C, electrons tend to transfer from ZIS to ZFO, and the CD at the interface between ZFO and ZIS can accelerate the transfer of e-, so the transfer speed of e- in C-ZFO@ZIS is faster. Moreover, the existence of CDs in C-ZFO@ZIS realizes multi-path transfer of photo-generated e-, accelerating e- migration and effectively promote the separation of photo-generated e- and h+ , thus efficaciously improving the photocatalytic performance of C-ZFO@ZIS. Based on the analysis of ESR test results, the ESR signal intensity of TEMPO − h+ , DMPO−•O2 − and DMPO−•OH for C-ZFO@ZIS are stronger than that of ZFO@ZIS-C, revealing that the number of h+ , •OH and •O2 − radicals on the C-ZFO@ZIS composite are larger than that of ZFO@ZIS-C. The ESR test results evidence that when the CDs is located on the surface of semiconductor material with narrow bandgap (ZFO) in type I heterojunction, it is more beneficial to the separation and migration of photo-generated carriers. The detailed photocatalytic reaction mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of TC with C-ZFO@ZIS is provided in the Fig. 8e, TC is converted into small molecular substances, H2O and CO2 under the action of h+ , •O2 − and •OH radicals [72–74]. The equation of photodegradation procedures for CDs-ZFO@ZIS is illustrated as below:

TC photodegradation pathway and the intermediates formed during the process were further investigated by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Fig.S16). Throughout the photodegradation process, intermediates are mainly produced through two channels: the loss or cleavage of specific functional groups and ring-opening reactions based on previous studies [75–80]. As presented in Fig. 9 , the product with m/z = 445 corresponds to the molecular weight of TC. For pathway I, TC molecules lost one methyl group of the dimethylamine group to transform to product 1 (m/z = 430), followed by the removal of another methyl group to product 2 (m/z = 402). Product 3 (m/z = 303) was derived by the open-ring and oxidation reaction of product 2, at the same time of demethylation, product 3 lost methanol to obtain product 4 (m/z = 260). TC molecule breaks N–CH3 and C–NH2 at the same time as losing a water molecule, resulting in product 5 (m/z = 395). Product 5 was converted into product 6 (m/z = 273) by ring opening reaction. Afterwards, product 6 lost methyl group and underwent successive open-ring reactions to generate product 7 (m/z = 192) and product 8 (m/z = 150). Ultimately, product 4 and product 6 were decomposed into CO2, H2O and other molecules by the collaborative efforts of active species.

In summary, two kinds of ZFO@ZIS heterojunctions (ZFO@ZIS-C and C-ZFO@ZIS) with anchoring CDs on the different positions were successfully constructed and the influence of the position of CDs in heterojunctions on the photocatalytic degradation of TC was investigated. After some series of targeted characterizations, the C-ZFO@ZIS ternary heterostructure that CDs anchoring on the core surface of core–shell structure (the contact interface of heterojunction) effectively enables the rapid separation and transfer of photo-generated charges in type I heterojunctions. As expected, the optimal 30-2C-ZFO@ZIS sample displays the best photocatalytic degradation activity (120 min, 30 mg/L TC degradation rate reached 87.3 %). This study provides a useful design idea for the construction of CDs-based heterojunction photocatalysts with excellent photocatalytic performance for water environment purification.
